---
title: "Calibration"
subtitle: "Are the fan bands honest?"
---

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
calibration = FileAttachment("_data/calibration.json").json()
Plot = import("https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/@observablehq/plot@0.6/+esm")
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof selectedCountry = Inputs.radio(
  ["norway", "canada", "sweden"],
  {label: "Country", value: "norway",
   format: v => ({"norway": "Norway", "canada": "Canada", "sweden": "Sweden"})[v]}
)

targetDisplay = ({
  "cpi": "CPI",
  "industrial_production": "Industrial production",
  "retail_sales": "Retail sales",
  "unemployment": "Unemployment"
})
horizonColors = ["#1f77b4", "#ff7f0e", "#2ca02c", "#d62728"]  // h=1,3,6,12
```

## Why bands matter

The Results page evaluates **point forecasts** under MASE. But most consumers
of macro forecasts care just as much about the *fan chart* as about the
central projection: a central bank does not want a single number, it wants
to know how likely it is that inflation lands above or below a policy-
relevant threshold. Chronos-2 emits a discrete predictive distribution at
each origin as five quantiles — q10, q25, q50, q75, q90 — which form nominal
**80 %** and **50 %** prediction bands.

Whether those bands are empirically calibrated — *do 80 % of realisations
actually fall inside the nominal 80 % band?* — is a separate question from
whether the median is accurate. This page answers it.

## How often do the bands actually cover?

Pooled across all country × target × horizon × test-era origin
combinations:

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
{
  const p = calibration.pooled;
  const fmt = v => v == null ? "–" : (v * 100).toFixed(1) + "%";
  const gap = v => v == null ? "" : ` (${v > 0 ? "+" : ""}${v.toFixed(1)} pp from nominal)`;
  return html`
  <table class="table" style="font-variant-numeric: tabular-nums; width:auto;">
    <thead>
      <tr>
        <th></th>
        <th>80 % band <small>(q10–q90)</small></th>
        <th>50 % band <small>(q25–q75)</small></th>
      </tr>
    </thead>
    <tbody>
      <tr><td><strong>Fine-tuned (raw)</strong></td>
          <td>${fmt(p.ft80)}${gap(p.ft80_gap)}</td>
          <td>${fmt(p.ft50)}${gap(p.ft50_gap)}</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Zero-shot</td>
          <td>${fmt(p.zs80)}${gap(p.zs80_gap)}</td>
          <td>${fmt(p.zs50)}${gap(p.zs50_gap)}</td></tr>
      <tr><td><strong>Fine-tuned + isotonic recalibration</strong></td>
          <td>${fmt(p.cal80)}${gap(p.cal80_gap)}</td>
          <td>${fmt(p.cal50)}${gap(p.cal50_gap)}</td></tr>
    </tbody>
  </table>
  `;
}
```

Two things jump out. First, the raw 80 % band catches only about 70 % of
realisations — a roughly ten-percentage-point shortfall. Second, **zero-
shot and fine-tuned variants are within a percentage point of one another**.
The variance compression is a property of the pretrained Chronos-2 model;
LoRA adaptation is not the culprit, and fine-tuning cannot be relied upon
to fix band calibration either.

### Coverage by target and horizon

Pick a country to inspect the per-target, per-horizon breakdown:

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
{
  const rows = calibration.coverage
    .filter(r => r.country === selectedCountry);
  const pairs = [
    ["cpi", "CPI"],
    ["industrial_production", "IP"],
    ["retail_sales", "Retail"],
    ["unemployment", "Unempl."]
  ];
  const fmt = v => v == null ? "" : v.toFixed(3);
  const targets = Array.from(new Set(rows.map(r => r.target)));
  const horizons = Array.from(new Set(rows.map(r => r.horizon))).sort((a, b) => a - b);

  // Build one table per band (80% and 50%) to keep columns manageable.
  const buildTable = (band, nominalText) => {
    const subset = rows.filter(r => r.band === band);
    const byKey = new Map();
    for (const r of subset) byKey.set(`${r.target}|${r.horizon}`, r);
    return html`
    <h4>${nominalText}% band</h4>
    <table class="table" style="font-variant-numeric: tabular-nums; font-size: 0.9em;">
      <thead>
        <tr>
          <th>Target</th>
          <th>h</th>
          <th>FT</th>
          <th>ZS</th>
          <th>Cal</th>
        </tr>
      </thead>
      <tbody>
        ${targets.flatMap(t => horizons.filter(h => [1, 3, 6, 12].includes(h)).map(h => {
          const r = byKey.get(`${t}|${h}`);
          return html`<tr>
            <td>${pairs.find(p => p[0] === t)?.[1] || t}</td>
            <td>${h}</td>
            <td>${r ? fmt(r.ft) : "–"}</td>
            <td>${r ? fmt(r.zs) : "–"}</td>
            <td>${r ? fmt(r.cal) : "–"}</td>
          </tr>`;
        }))}
      </tbody>
    </table>
    `;
  };

  return html`<div style="display:flex; gap: 2em; flex-wrap: wrap;">
    <div>${buildTable("80", "80")}</div>
    <div>${buildTable("50", "50")}</div>
  </div>`;
}
```

Notice that the **Cal** column closes most of the gap in the 50 % band — it
often lands within a few percentage points of the nominal 0.500 — but the
80 % band improves only modestly. Because the base forecast only emits five
quantiles, the empirical CDF used to recalibrate saturates at τ = 0.1 and
τ = 0.9; there is no base-model support beyond those points for the
calibrator to stretch into. A denser base quantile grid would lift that
ceiling.

## Where does the mass go?

The PIT (probability-integral-transform) histogram shows which regions of
the predictive distribution the actuals land in. Under perfect calibration
the histogram is flat, with density 1. A U-shape means variance compression
(actuals fall in the tails too often). An **asymmetric** U-shape means the
model is systematically biased — under-predicting if right-heavy,
over-predicting if left-heavy.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
{
  const rows = calibration.pit_bins
    .filter(r => r.country === selectedCountry);
  if (rows.length === 0) return html`<em>No data</em>`;
  return Plot.plot({
    height: 260,
    marginLeft: 55,
    marginRight: 20,
    marginBottom: 45,
    marginTop: 25,
    facet: {data: rows, x: "target", label: null},
    fx: {tickFormat: t => targetDisplay[t] || t, label: null},
    x: {label: "PIT value", domain: [0, 1], ticks: [0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1]},
    y: {label: "Density", grid: true, domain: [0, 3.5]},
    marks: [
      Plot.rectY(rows, {
        x1: "bin_lo",
        x2: "bin_hi",
        y2: "density",
        fill: d => d.is_tail ? "#d62728" : "#aec7e8",
        stroke: "#333",
        strokeWidth: 0.6,
        opacity: 0.85
      }),
      Plot.ruleY([1.0], {stroke: "#555", strokeDasharray: "4,3"}),
      Plot.frame()
    ]
  });
}
```

Per-target tail fractions under **zero-shot** Chronos-2 (expected 10 % in
each tail under perfect calibration):

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
{
  const rows = calibration.directional_bias
    .filter(r => r.country === selectedCountry);
  const fmt = v => (v * 100).toFixed(1) + "%";
  const highlight = v => Math.abs(v - 0.1) > 0.05 ? "font-weight:bold;color:#c0392b;" : "";
  return html`
  <table class="table" style="font-variant-numeric: tabular-nums;">
    <thead>
      <tr>
        <th>Target</th>
        <th>Below q10</th>
        <th>Inside 80 % band</th>
        <th>Above q90</th>
        <th>n</th>
      </tr>
    </thead>
    <tbody>
      ${rows.map(r => html`<tr>
        <td>${targetDisplay[r.target] || r.target}</td>
        <td style="${highlight(r.below_q10)}">${fmt(r.below_q10)}</td>
        <td>${fmt(r.within)}</td>
        <td style="${highlight(r.above_q90)}">${fmt(r.above_q90)}</td>
        <td>${r.n}</td>
      </tr>`)}
    </tbody>
  </table>
  `;
}
```

Three distinct patterns appear across the panel. Canada retail sales places
a very large share of realisations above q90, consistent with Chronos-2
under-predicting Canadian retail trade on the 2021–2024 rebound. Canada
unemployment is the mirror: realisations cluster below q10 because the
2020 spike in joblessness and subsequent tightening outpaced the model.
Some series — Norway CPI most cleanly — are nearly symmetric, indicating
pure variance compression with no directional tilt.

## Empirical versus nominal

A calibration curve plots the empirical fraction of realisations at or
below the predicted τ-quantile, against the nominal τ. Perfect
calibration traces the 45-degree diagonal. The fine-tuned Chronos-2
forecast shows the characteristic S-shape of a too-narrow distribution:
above the diagonal at low τ (too many actuals land below q10) and below
the diagonal at high τ (too many land above q90).

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
{
  const rows = calibration.calibration_curve
    .filter(r => r.country === selectedCountry)
    .filter(r => [1, 3, 6, 12].includes(r.horizon));
  if (rows.length === 0) return html`<em>No data</em>`;
  return Plot.plot({
    height: 280,
    marginLeft: 60,
    marginBottom: 45,
    marginTop: 25,
    facet: {data: rows, x: "target", label: null},
    fx: {tickFormat: t => targetDisplay[t] || t, label: null},
    x: {label: "Nominal τ", domain: [0, 1], ticks: [0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1]},
    y: {label: "Empirical P(Y ≤ Q(τ))", domain: [0, 1], grid: true},
    color: {
      domain: [1, 3, 6, 12],
      range: horizonColors,
      legend: true,
      label: "Horizon"
    },
    marks: [
      Plot.ruleY([0], {stroke: "#ddd"}),
      Plot.link([{}], {x1: 0, y1: 0, x2: 1, y2: 1,
                      stroke: "#888", strokeDasharray: "4,3"}),
      Plot.line(rows, {x: "nominal", y: "empirical_ft",
                       stroke: "horizon", strokeWidth: 1.5, curve: "monotone-x"}),
      Plot.dot(rows, {x: "nominal", y: "empirical_ft",
                      fill: "horizon", r: 3.5, tip: true}),
      Plot.frame()
    ]
  });
}
```

Curves that deviate more aggressively from the diagonal indicate stronger
miscalibration. In most targets the departure is largest at the shortest
horizon (h = 1): the model is most overconfident about its next-month
prediction.

## Post-hoc isotonic recalibration

We apply a lightweight per-series **isotonic recalibration** of the
predictive quantiles. For each (country, target, horizon) triple we
compute PIT values on the validation era (2006–2015) and take their
empirical CDF `F̂`. At test time the calibrated quantile at level τ is
`Q_cal(τ) = Q_base(F̂⁻¹(τ))`, where `Q_base` is the linearly interpolated
base quantile function. The procedure is distribution-free, uses
validation-era residuals only — so it respects the pseudo-real-time
discipline — and requires no retraining of the foundation model. It is
implemented in `src/calibration.py`.

As shown in the table at the top of the page, the aggregate effect of
recalibration is to move the pooled 80 % band empirical coverage from
**69.5 %** to **72.5 %** and the pooled 50 % band from **41.6 %** to
**47.3 %**. Per-series, improvements are largest where validation-era
and test-era PIT distributions resemble one another; they can even be
negative under regime change (notably, Canada unemployment loses about
eight percentage points of 80 % coverage after recalibration because the
validation era reflects a gradually tightening labour market while the
test era includes the 2020–2021 shock).

## What's left

Two limits bound how far this recalibration can go. The structural limit
is the five-knot base quantile function: no post-hoc layer can reach mass
beyond q10 or q90 that the base model did not emit. The second limit is
regime stability — a calibrator fit to one era carries a regime-shift
risk that no method blind to structural breaks can resolve. For the
deeper methodological discussion, see paper §5.8 and Appendix D.
